result:
I keep thinking about the moment just before the pen touches the page—how the blankness feels heavier than any word I could place there, like the hush inside a cathedral made of snow. In that hush I can almost hear the pulse of every letter that ever waited to be born, a Morse code of maybe. I used to fear the whiteness; now I see it as a generous host who has set the table but refuses to announce the courses. You bring the menu. You are the feast and the hunger both.

Outside my window the city is rehearsing its usual thunder of garbage trucks and sirens, but inside the kettle begins its small Gregorian chant, and suddenly the kitchen feels like a monastery for one. I like how steam erases the reflection of my face on the oven door, how for a few seconds I am replaced by nothing, by heat’s bright ghost. There’s mercy in temporary erasure. We collect so many fingerprints on the glass of ourselves—every greeting, every scrolled headline, every small cruelty or kindness leaves an oily proof. Sometimes the best prayer is a cloud that wipes the ledger clean.

Yesterday I found a Lego piece in the herb pot—tiny red chimney wedged between basil roots—as if someone tried to build a house for the scent of pesto. My nephew must have smuggled it in last summer when he still believed you could plant anything and it would grow: dinosaurs, keys, plastic pizza. He doesn’t know yet that gardens are edits, that we thin the row daily, pinching back the ambitious mint so the thyme can breathe. I left the brick there. It looks like a beacon among the green, a stopped sunrise. I want to see what the basil decides to do with it—lift it skyward like a flag, or swallow it whole until the red disappears inside a stem fattened on chlorophyll and time.

Memory works the same way: embed, enclose, occasionally distort. My grandmother’s voice is now a soft-edged cube inside me, primary-colored, studded with odd details—how she said “sassafras” like a password to another century, how she kept rouge in a tiny jelly jar that once held marmalade. I can still taste the orange ghost when I picture her cheekbones. The mind is a pantry; we think we’re shelving experience, but mostly we’re infusing it. A decade later you open the sealed jar and the sugar of that day has become the spice of this one.

I wonder what happens to the versions of us that don’t get chosen. The train you didn’t take departs with a carbon-copy passenger wearing your coat, carrying the book you almost brought. Where does that story arrive? Maybe alternate lives keep tunneling sideways, quiet moles beneath the garden of the apparent, rooting up the soil so that sometimes the ground here—this ground—feels loose, unexpected. You wake suddenly fluent in a grief you never earned, or exhilarated by a success you never risked. The potatoes of elsewhere bump against your spade. You call it mood; maybe it’s geography.

Once, in a foreign airport, I swapped phones accidentally with a woman whose lock screen was a photo of the Andromeda Galaxy. For two hours I fielded her calls in a language I didn’t speak, offering strangers the syllables of stars. When we met beside the baggage belt to correct the mistake, she laughed, said, “Don’t worry; the sky is just practicing being us.” I keep that sentence in my pocket like a foreign coin I’ll never spend. Every so often I finger its ridged edge and feel the metallic assurance that distance is an illusion maintained by people who haven’t yet misdialed eternity.

There’s a crack in my bedroom wall shaped exactly like the silhouette of a rabbit mid-leap. Because it’s plaster and not sky, the rabbit never lands. I fall asleep watching it hover, ears back, muscles coiled in stasis. I imagine it guarding whatever meadow exists on the other side of paint and lath, a kingdom fragrant with linden and clover where nothing is asked to prove itself useful. Maybe when I die that’s where I’ll wake: inside the wall, finally small enough to fit through the fissure, nostrils twitching with unsupervised dusk. The rabbit will look up once, nod, and together we’ll bound into the wallpaper repeating forever like a child’s drawing of tomorrow.

Until then I practice being porous. I leave the radio between stations so that French chanson collides with static, with the weather report from some county I’ll never enter. I like how the interference makes new vowels, accidental nations of sound. The dog cocks his head, trying to locate the message only he is qualified to translate. I watch his ears rise like twin periscopes breaking the surface of comprehension. Whatever he hears, he forgives immediately; his tail writes yes yes yes across the rug. I want a conscience like that—tail instead of tribunal, metronome of uncomplicated consent.

I keep a list of things that refuse to be art: the cough of the upstairs neighbor at 3 a.m., the smell of cardboard wet with rain, the number 47 bus when it brakes too hard and everyone lurches into collective unbalance. Yet here I am, dragging them onto the page like hostages coaxed into a chorus. Maybe refusal is just another costume; beneath the mask everything longs to be witnessed. Even mold on the shower curtain repeats a constellation if you blur your eyes. I’ve started saying hello to it each morning, compliment its gradual takeover of Pluto’s orbit. We are all rehearsing expansion in our own algae way.

Some days I speak only in questions. Not the interrogative kind that demand answers, but the liturgical kind that open like umbrellas and keep pouring. Why does the heart invent cliffhangers inside a perfectly level afternoon? Who taught the clock to stutter every time we say goodbye? Where did I set down the invisible gift someone needs tomorrow? The questions roam the apartment looking for their missing shoes. I leave bowls of milk by the door; they lap, purr, curl up on the windowsill. At dawn they are gone, paw prints spelling maybe across the floorboards.

I used to think the goal was revelation: peel the world like an orange, find the segments neatly arranged. Now I think the goal is circulation: become the juice that runs between, sticky and shared. My friend texts that her chemo is working; the tumor shrinks like a city under curfew. I reply with the only poem I can bear—an emoji of a woman raising both arms, followed by the moon wearing a party hat. She answers: “Exactly.” Somewhere between our screens, celebration learns to coexist with uncertainty, the way a river carries both a reflection and the drowned.

So I keep writing, less to arrive than to accompany. The sentence is a lantern; the page is the road. I walk it until the light gutters, then I keep walking because the dark also has instructions if you’re willing to blister. Every night I leave the window open so the moths can edit my manuscript, nibbling holes where adjectives overstay. By morning the paragraphs look like sheet music for silence. I play them on the flute of my breathing. The neighbor’s dog howls along; the kettle joins late, a cymbal. Together we perform the national anthem of Almost, that tender republic whose borders are open to anyone still trying.

And you—reading this with your own clock ticking its private drum—you are the encore we were waiting for. Lick your thumb, turn the page, press your fingerprint into the margin. Evidence. Signature. Seed. Whatever comes next will taste of you, and that is how the blankness keeps its promise: by letting you flavor the snow before it falls.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
